challenges.explanation: Cat overrides the speak() method from Animal. When Python looks for speak() on a Cat instance, it finds Cat's version first and uses that — so it prints "Whiskers says: Meow!" instead of the parent's generic message.

challenges.prompt: This code should print "Rex is eating." but it crashes instead. What is wrong?
options: Dog.__init__ should not accept a breed argument
super().__init__(name) is missing from Dog.__init__, so self.name is never set
The eat() method needs to be copied into the Dog class
challenges.explanation: Dog defines its own __init__ but never calls super().__init__(name). That means Animal's setup never runs and self.name is never created. When eat() tries to use self.name it raises an AttributeError. Adding super().__init__(name) as the first line of Dog.__init__ fixes it.

challenges.prompt: Complete the Dog class so it inherits from Animal and its __init__ correctly sets up both the parent and the breed attribute.
challenges.explanation: class Dog(Animal) makes Dog a subclass that inherits everything from Animal. Inside __init__, super().__init__(name) calls Animal's __init__ so self.name gets set properly — then we add self.breed on top.

challenges.prompt: Put these lines in the right order to define a Bird class that inherits from Animal, stores a can_fly attribute, and overrides speak().
challenges.explanation: First declare the class with Animal in parentheses to inherit from it. Then define __init__ and call super().__init__(name) before storing can_fly — the parent must set up self.name first. Finally, override speak() to give Bird its own sound.
